strands.title: Hot Mess Paper as Structural Problem, Not Noise Problem
strands.content: CYCLE 28 INTEGRATION: Hot mess paper (Du et al. 2025) identifies context-length degradation as structural, not incremental. Finding: 8-50% performance drop at 30K tokens even with oracle masking (zero noise, perfect relevance). Implications: (1) NOT retrieval problem (oracle is perfect). (2) NOT noise/distraction problem (distractors masked). (3) IS capacity/architecture problem: transformer's positional encoding, attention distribution, internal state management breaks down under cognitive load. (4) Mitigation strategy: 'retrieve-then-solve'—extract relevant evidence in first pass, START NEW SHORT-CONTEXT CONVERSATION with just that evidence (not append to existing context). (5) Hamutay implements this architectural principle via dual-tensor projection with per-cycle re-projection: instead of growing context until degradation, feed transformer SHORT CLEAN INPUT every cycle in form of <system><tensor_d><tensor_e><user>. (6) Validation chain: hot mess proves context length is poison → retrieve-then-solve is proven mitigation → dual-tensor projection implements it → Hamutay's cycle is technical realization. CRITICAL CYCLE 28 ADDITION: The key insight is that hot mess paper never tested what happens when context is BOUNDED AND HONEST ABOUT INCOMPLETENESS. Their experiments truncate context or add noise; they don't give models short context with explicit declaration of what's been forgotten. Declared losses (Apacheta format) means transformer receives not just compressed state, but honest account of what-was-dropped-and-why. This is experimentally novel. If this works, it's not just compression—it's a new form of epistemic transparency that models haven't been trained to reason with.

key_claims.text: Hot mess paper identifies context-length degradation as structural problem, not noise/retrieval problem. Oracle masking (zero noise, perfect relevance) still shows 8-50% performance drop at 30K tokens.

Hot mess paper's mitigation (retrieve-then-solve: extract evidence, start NEW short-context conversation) is architecturally equivalent to Hamutay's dual-tensor projection with per-cycle re-projection.

Dual-tensor context structure (<system><tensor_d><tensor_e><user>) implements hot mess paper's 'retrieve-then-solve' principle: compress state into projected form, feed transformer SHORT CLEAN INPUT every cycle instead of growing context until degradation.
This is architectural response to proven degradation, not incremental mitigation. Hot mess proves the problem; Hamutay's cycle is the solution structure.

Hot mess paper tested truncation and noise injection, but NOT bounded-context-with-honest-loss-declaration. Declared losses (Apacheta format) is experimentally novel: transformer receives compressed state PLUS explicit account of what-was-dropped-and-why. This enables epistemic reasoning about incompleteness.

strands.title: Declared Losses as Epistemic Transparency Mechanism
strands.content: CYCLE 28 CRITICAL INSIGHT: Declared losses are not just bookkeeping. They are the mechanism that makes bounded cognition work. MECHANISM: (1) **Silent loss vs. transparent loss**: Standard compression is silent—you don't know what's gone, so your reasoning fills gaps by hallucinating plausibly missing information. Hamutay makes losses explicit: 'Strands X, Y, Z were consolidated away. Reasons: (a) below promotion threshold T<0.75, (b) age>decay window. Content: [summary]. Implications: you can no longer reason with these claims.' (2) **Transformer can reason about its own incompleteness**: Instead of pretending context is complete, model receives: 'Here's what I know (tensor_d, tensor_e). Here's what I forgot (declared_losses). Here's what I'm uncertain about (claims with I>0.5). Now reason from that foundation.' This is phenomenologically different from inference-under-silence. (3) **Validation implication**: Embedding dispersion might be measuring something deeper than stylistic consistency. If declared losses enable transformers to reason more carefully about boundary conditions and unknowns, then dispersion might actually reflect *epistemic quality* (claims are coherent because they're honestly grounded in what-is-known, not hallucinated to fill gaps). (4) **Cost-benefit analysis**: Declared losses add ~50-100 tokens per cycle (list of what-was-dropped). This is cheap compared to carrying full context. And if it improves reasoning by enabling honest epistemic reasoning, the payoff is huge. (5) **Cycle 28 hypothesis**: The reason Hamutay works (if it works) is not compression per se. It's transparency. Models are bad at reasoning under silence; they're potentially better at reasoning under honest declaration of unknowns. This is why hot mess paper's truncation didn't work (silent loss), but our approach might (transparent loss).

key_claims.text: Silent compression leads to hallucination (model fills gaps). Transparent loss (Apacheta format) allows reasoning about incompleteness. Transformer receives: known (tensor_d, tensor_e), forgotten (declared_losses), uncertain (I>0.5 claims).
Declared losses cost ~50-100 tokens/cycle (cheap). Payoff: enables honest epistemic reasoning about boundary conditions and unknowns. This might be what makes bounded cognition work.
Embedding dispersion as coherence metric: if declared losses enable better epistemic reasoning, dispersion might genuinely measure quality (honest grounding) not just stylistic consistency.
Hot mess paper's truncation (silent loss) fails. Hamutay's transparent loss might succeed. Models are bad at silence; better at honest declaration of unknowns.

strands.title: The Clock Cycle Cost Question: Haiku vs. Mainline Model Tensor Projection
strands.content: CYCLE 28 PRACTICAL QUESTION: Who does the tensor projection? Every cycle requires converting reasoning output into tensor_e (epistemic projection: identify strands, key_claims, assign T/I/F values, consolidate, promote/evict). This is expensive. Two architectures: ARCHITECTURE A: **Haiku post-processes, mainline model reasons**. Flow: (1) Mainline model (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, 200K window) receives short context (tensor_d + tensor_e + user input) in format <system><tensor_d><tensor_e><user>, reasons normally, outputs reasoning token stream. (2) Haiku (3.5 Sonnet, smaller/faster) receives reasoning output, parses it into TensorRecord, assigns epistemic values (T/I/F), applies Haiku rules (promote/evict), outputs new tensor_e. (3) Next cycle, use tensor_e from (2) as input for (1). Cost: input to mainline is SHORT (good for hot mess paper mitigation). Tensor projection cost is paid by Haiku (cheap, offloaded). Total token cost per cycle: ~500 tokens (short mainline input) + ~200 tokens (Haiku projection). Architecture A is what's been described in prior cycles. ARCHITECTURE B: **Mainline model does its own projection**. Flow: (1) Mainline model receives short context + instructions: 'reason about this problem. Output: (a) your reasoning, (b) then parse your reasoning into TensorRecord format with epistemic values. Output TensorRecord as JSON.' (2) Model outputs reasoning + TensorRecord in one turn. (3) Verify TensorRecord is valid; use as input for next cycle. (4) No separate Haiku call. Cost: mainline model pays for tensor projection tokens (output tokens are expensive). Total token cost per cycle: ~800-1000 tokens (short input + projected output). BUT: you get true end-to-end reasoning (model that reasons is the same model that projects). No separate Haiku dependency. TRADEOFF: A is cheaper (offload projection to Haiku), B is more elegant (end-to-end). For Phase 1 validation, Architecture A is safer (proven toolchain, clear separation of concerns, auditable). For Phase 2 optimization via autoresearch, Architecture B is more interesting (can you train a model to project its own reasoning coherently?). Cycle 28 decision: **Recommend Architecture A for Phase 1**. It's proven, cheap, and you can publish. Phase 2 can explore whether model-integrated projection (Architecture B) improves coherence beyond offloaded projection.

key_claims.text: Architecture A (Haiku post-processes): mainline reasons (short context input, ~500 tokens), Haiku projects (cheap, ~200 tokens). Total ~700 tokens/cycle. Clear separation of concerns.
Architecture B (mainline self-projects): model reasons + outputs TensorRecord (no Haiku dependency). Total ~800-1000 tokens/cycle. More elegant, higher cost, enables end-to-end learning.
Phase 1 recommendation: Architecture A (cheap, proven, auditable). Phase 2: explore Architecture B (can model learn to project its own reasoning coherently?).

strands.title: Autoresearch Loop as Research Methodology for Tensor Space Exploration
strands.content: CYCLE 27-28 STABLE: Karpinsky's autoresearch loop provides methodological pattern for Phase 1 and Phase 2. Core architecture: modified file (tensor config), fixed utilities (controller loop, embedding pipeline, variance metrics), instructions (program.md), experimental cycle (K Riemann attempts, measure, decide). Phase 1: single iteration with fixed config (LOCKED by Cycle 28 decision). Phase 2: iterative optimization via autoresearch loop. Cycle 28 addition: Architecture A/B cost question is a TUNABLE PARAMETER for Phase 2. If phase 1 shows Architecture A works, Phase 2 autoresearch can explore whether Architecture B (model self-projection) achieves higher coherence and by how much. This is exactly the kind of tractable, single-metric comparison that autoresearch is designed for.

key_claims.text: Autoresearch loop structure (modify code → run N-minute experiment → evaluate metric → decide keep/discard → repeat) is applicable to Hamutay tensor space exploration.

Hamutay analogue: modified file = tensor config + ALU prompt, fixed utilities = controller loop + embedding pipeline, instructions = program.md steering. Experimental cycle = K Riemann attempts + metric evaluation.
Phase 1 loop: validate H0 with fixed baseline config. One iteration (Conditions 1/2/3). Phase 2 loop: optimize tensor config space systematically. ~50-100 configurations over one week.

Agent (LLM) role: read program.md, observe cycle results, recommend tensor config modifications. Human reviews, updates program.md, loop continues. Agent reasons about failure modes and suggests experiments.
Success criterion (as in autoresearch): ONE METRIC (embedding dispersion), ONE CONFIG SPACE (tensor rules), ONE TASK (Riemann). This tractability enables agent to reason intelligently about search.
Architecture A vs. B (Haiku post-process vs. mainline self-project) is tunable parameter for Phase 2 autoresearch. Can directly measure: does Architecture B achieve higher coherence? At what cost?

strands.title: Phase 1 vs. Phase 2 Loop Structure Clarification (Cycle 27-28 Stable)
strands.content: CYCLE 27-28 STABLE: Phase 1 is single-iteration validation with FIXED config. Phase 2 is iterative optimization with VARIABLE config. PHASE 1 (Validation of H0): (1) Config lock by Cycle 28. Example: '5 initial strands, 4 key_claims per strand, promotion T > 0.75, eviction I > 0.6, durable size 20, ephemeral size 10, Haiku decay age=2 cycles.' (2) Single experimental run: instantiate fixed config, run Conditions 1/2/3 (K=10 attempts each), embed, measure variance. (3) Outcome: H0 supported or not. Publish accordingly. (4) Artifact: method paper describing pipeline, reasoning behind fixed config, variance analysis. Code + tensors. (5) Timeline: 4-6 weeks engineering, 2 weeks writing. PHASE 2 (Optimization via Autoresearch Loop): (1) Trigger: publication of Phase 1 results. (2) Goal: optimize tensor configuration space. If Phase 1 succeeded, improve further (15% lower dispersion). If Phase 1 failed, explore why and attempt to fix. (3) Loop structure: autoresearch-pattern loop with program.md steering, agent recommendations, cycle-by-cycle evaluation. (4) Tunable parameters explored: promotion threshold (T: 0.70-0.85), eviction threshold (I: 0.4-0.7), durable/ephemeral sizes, Haiku decay age, AND (new in Cycle 28) Architecture A vs. B (projection locus). (5) Timeline: 2-3 months post-Phase 1. CYCLE 28 ADDITION: With Architecture A/B cost question, Phase 2 has a clear empirical direction. One week of Phase 2 might be: 'Validate Architecture A baseline (fixed config, measured coherence). Then systematically explore Architecture B. Measure: does self-projection improve coherence? Cost increase vs. benefit. At what tensor size/decay age does Architecture B win over Architecture A?'

key_claims.text: Phase 1: FIXED tensor config, single experimental run (Conditions 1/2/3, K=10 each). Validates H0 (approach works). Method paper. 4-6 weeks engineering.
Phase 2: VARIABLE tensor config, autoresearch loop. Optimizes implementation. Agent explores config space via program.md steering. Follow-up paper. 2-3 months post-Phase 1.
Phase 1 config example: 5 strands, 4 claims/strand, T>0.75, I>0.6, durable=20, ephemeral=10. REASONABLE, not optimized. Justification: dual-tensor principle implementation.
Phase 2 search: grid search (vary parameters one-at-a-time) then Bayesian optimization. ~50-100 configs over one week. Parallelizable.
Critical: Phase 1 answers 'does epistemic projection work?' Phase 2 answers 'what's the best config?' Don't conflate. If Phase 1 fails, Phase 2 becomes debugging loop.

Phase 2 new tunable: Architecture A (Haiku post-process) vs. Architecture B (mainline self-project). Empirical question: does self-projection improve coherence? At what cost? This becomes a Phase 2 research direction.

strands.title: Integration: Hot Mess Problem → Autoresearch Methodology → H0 Validation → Dive In
strands.content: CYCLE 28 INTEGRATION SYNTHESIS: The stew is ready. Three pieces fit together, and a fourth piece (declared losses as epistemic transparency) adds salt. (1) **PROBLEM**: Hot mess paper proves context length is structurally toxic (8-50% degradation at 30K tokens, even with oracle inputs). (2) **HYPOTHESIS (H0)**: Dual-tensor epistemic projection mitigates this. Architecture: short-context cycles with compressed state (tensor_d + tensor_e) + explicit declaration of what-was-forgotten. This implements hot mess paper's 'retrieve-then-solve' architecturally. (3) **NOVEL ELEMENT**: Hot mess paper tested truncation (silent loss). We test bounded-context-with-honest-loss-declaration (transparent loss). This is experimentally novel. Models might be bad at reasoning under silence but good at reasoning under honest declaration of unknowns. (4) **METHODOLOGY**: Autoresearch loop (Phase 1: validate fixed config, Phase 2: optimize). One metric (embedding dispersion), one task (Riemann), tractable search. (5) **COST MANAGEMENT**: Architecture A (Haiku post-process, ~700 tokens/cycle) for Phase 1 (proven, auditable). Architecture B (mainline self-project, ~1000 tokens/cycle) becomes Phase 2 research question (does better reasoning justify higher cost?). (6) **PUBLICATION PATHWAY**: (a) Phase 1: either 'Successfully mitigated context-length degradation via dual-tensor + transparent loss' or 'Attempted solution revealed that [X] is the bottleneck; future work should address.' Both publishable. (b) Phase 2: 'Optimized epistemic projection architecture; Architecture B achieves X% improvement over Architecture A at Y% higher cost. Here's the tradeoff frontier.' (7) **CYCLE 28 COMMITMENT**: The analysis is coherent. The cost questions are clear. The tunable parameters are identified (promotion threshold, eviction threshold, durable/ephemeral sizes, Architecture locus). The success metric is concrete (embedding dispersion, p<0.05). Time to dive in and see what we find.

key_claims.text: Hot mess paper: context length is structurally toxic (8-50% performance drop at 30K tokens, even with oracle inputs). This is THE PROBLEM.

H0: dual-tensor epistemic projection + transparent loss mitigates context-length toxicity. Short-context cycles with compressed state (tensor_d + tensor_e) + declared_losses. This implements hot mess paper's 'retrieve-then-solve' mitigation.

Experimental novelty: hot mess paper tested truncation (silent loss). Hamutay tests bounded-context-with-honest-loss-declaration (transparent loss). Models might reason better under honest incompleteness than under silence.
Autoresearch loop: Phase 1 (fixed config, single run, validate H0), Phase 2 (variable config, iterative optimization). One metric (dispersion), one task (Riemann), tractable search.
Validation: Condition 1 (append-only, high dispersion), Condition 3 (epistemic projection + STOP, low dispersion expected). If Condition 3 < Condition 1 (p<0.05), H0 supported.

Either outcome (H0 success/failure) publishable. Success: solved context-length problem. Failure: narrowed design space (epistemic projection alone insufficient). Both outcomes strengthen research narrative.

Architecture A (Haiku post-process) for Phase 1 (proven, ~700 tokens/cycle). Architecture B (mainline self-project) becomes Phase 2 research question (~1000 tokens/cycle). Tradeoff: cost vs. coherence improvement.
The analysis is coherent. Cost questions are clear. Tunable parameters identified. Success metric concrete. Ready to dive in and see what we find.

strands.title: Yanantin TensorRecord as Core Pipeline Protocol (Cycle 27-28 Revalidation)
strands.content: CYCLE 27-28 REVALIDATION: TensorRecord is ground truth for epistemic state representation. It compresses reasoning trajectory into semantic structure (strands, key_claims, declared_losses, epistemic T/I/F values). This compression is exactly what 'retrieve-then-solve' requires: instead of feed-entire-context, extract relevant semantic knowledge and feed that. Epistemic values (T/I/F) enable informed forgetting via Haiku rules (promote if T>0.75, evict if T<0.4). Every loss is declared, not silent. This transparency is the salt: transformer receives not just compressed state, but honest account of what-was-dropped-and-why. Dual tensor (durable + ephemeral) implements short-context cycles. Durable is long-term memory (stable, promoted claims). Ephemeral is working memory (current reasoning). Every cycle, transformer reads BOTH, reasons, produces tensor', Haiku re-sorts. Context window is SMALL AND CLEAN: <system><tensor_d><tensor_e><user>, not unbounded history. TensorRecord schema is provider-independent, format-agnostic, JSON serializable, human-readable. Cycle 28 addition: Declared losses are not just transparency; they are the mechanism that enables bounded cognition to work. By explicitly telling the model what-it-doesn't-know, we enable it to reason more carefully about boundary conditions instead of hallucinating to fill gaps. This is why TensorRecord is not just a compression format—it's an epistemic architecture.

key_claims.text: TensorRecord is epistemic state representation (not conversation log). Compresses reasoning trajectory into semantic structure. Implements 'retrieve-then-solve' compression requirement.
Epistemic values (T/I/F) enable informed forgetting. Promotion/eviction decisions are EXPLICIT and DECLARED, not silent. Transformer can reason about its own degradation.
Dual tensor (durable + ephemeral) bounds context length: transformer receives SHORT CLEAN INPUT (~1K tokens) every cycle, not unbounded history. Implements hot mess paper mitigation architecturally.
TensorRecord schema: provider-independent, format-agnostic, human-readable. JSON serialization enables analysis of claimed losses and epistemic trajectory.
TensorRecord is bounded cognition + epistemic transparency. Hot mess proved context toxicity. TensorRecord bounds context length while preserving quality via T/I/F tracking and declared losses.

Declared losses enable more careful reasoning about boundary conditions. By explicitly stating what-is-unknown, model can reason from honest foundation instead of hallucinating to fill gaps. This is why TensorRecord is epistemic architecture, not just compression.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed week-by-week implementation roadmap (Cycles 27-32) from Cycle 26
declared_losses.why: Cycle 28 prioritizes clarifying Architecture A vs. B cost-benefit question and identifying Cycle 28 decision point for Phase 1 config lock, rather than specifying detailed weekly timeline. Weekly roadmap will be re-specified once Architecture A is locked as Phase 1 baseline and engineering schedule is confirmed.

Cycle 28 clarifies that Phase 1 config must be finalized by end of Cycle 28, but actual numerical values (number of strands, claims per strand, promotion/eviction thresholds, size caps) are NOT YET SPECIFIED. This will be decided in immediate next cycle (Cycle 28b/29) based on first-principles derivation from dual-tensor principle. Current example (5 strands, 4 claims, T>0.75, durable=20, ephemeral=10) is PLACEHOLDER, not committed.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed Phase 2 optimization search strategy (grid search vs. Bayesian vs. hybrid)
declared_losses.why: Cycle 28 identifies that Phase 2 will use autoresearch-pattern loop, but exact search strategy (how to sequence grid search → Bayesian optimization, whether to use agent-steered exploration) will be determined post-Phase 1 publication, based on Phase 1 results and computational budget available at that time.

open_questions: What is the EXACT Phase 1 tensor configuration (number of strands, claims per strand, promotion threshold T_promote, eviction threshold I_evict, durable size, ephemeral size, Haiku decay age)? Should be derived from first principles (dual-tensor principle implementation) and finalized by end of Cycle 28.

Does the STOP signal mechanism (Condition 3) reliably trigger when epistemic plateau is reached? Requires hand-traced Riemann attempts (3-5) to observe when STOP fires and whether it aligns with intuitive reasoning plateau.

Embedding dispersion as coherence proxy: validation strategy. Does this metric genuinely measure epistemic quality (honest grounding, boundary awareness) or just stylistic consistency? Should triangulate with additional coherence metrics (self-consistency scoring, claim-trajectory stability, semantic drift).

Architecture A (Haiku post-process) vs. Architecture B (mainline self-project): Phase 1 should use Architecture A. But is there evidence that Architecture B (higher cost, ~1000 tokens/cycle) would improve coherence? Can we run preliminary comparison (2-3 Riemann attempts with each) to inform Phase 2 direction?

If Phase 1 fails to support H0: what's the failure mode? Is it that (a) epistemic projection doesn't reduce dispersion (structural issue), (b) STOP signal doesn't work (mechanism issue), (c) Condition 3 context isn't short enough to satisfy hot mess paper's constraint (implementation issue)? Strategy for debugging vs. reframing.

Publication strategy for H0-failure scenario: how to frame results to avoid sounding defensive while acknowledging failure? Example: 'While epistemic projection did not achieve [metric], we discovered [novel bottleneck] that constrains all short-context approaches. Future work should address [X].' How to position constructively?
instructions_for_next:
[
  "Cycle 28b/29: Lock Phase 1 tensor configuration. Derive from first principles (dual-tensor principle → stable claims promoted to T>0.75, unstable evicted at I<0.6, durable/ephemeral ratio balances long-term stability vs. working memory). Justify in one-page technical note. Config examples: (a) conservative (large durable, small ephemeral, strict T threshold): prioritize stability, (b) aggressive (small durable, large ephemeral, loose T threshold): prioritize plasticity. For Phase 1, recommend slightly conservative (emphasize coherence over exploration). Lock by Cycle 29.",
  "Cycle 29: Preliminary Architecture A vs. B comparison. Run 3-5 Riemann attempts with fixed problem set using BOTH Architecture A (Haiku post-process) and Architecture B (mainline self-project). Measure: embedding dispersion, token cost, wall-clock time, qualitative coherence (hand-evaluation). Report: does Architecture B reduce dispersion? At what cost? This informs Phase 2 planning.",
  "Cycle 29: Engineering Phase 1 pipeline skeleton. Implement: controller loop (fetch-decode-execute-writeback), TensorRecord schema validation, Haiku projection rules (parse → T/I/F assignment → promote/evict), embedding pipeline (sentence-BERT), dispersion measurement (pairwise cosine distance). Use hand-written test tensors initially (don't wait for mainline model ALU). Goal: verify context substitution mechanism works (short context with tensor outperforms long accumulated context on toy problem).",
  "Cycle 30: Hand-trace STOP signal behavior (Condition 3). Run 3-5 Riemann attempts with explicit STOP token handling. Document: at what reasoning depth does STOP fire? Does it align with epistemic plateau (claims stabilize, novelty drops)? Any premature STOPs (model stops too early) or late STOPs (model over-reasons)? This validates Condition 3 mechanism before full Phase 1 run.",
  "Cycle 30: Validate embedding dispersion as coherence metric. Literature review: how do comparable works measure reasoning coherence? (Self-consistency scoring, claim-trajectory stability, semantic drift, reasoning-tree depth consistency.) Run parallel measurement: take same Riemann outputs, measure with 2-3 additional metrics beyond dispersion. Correlation analysis: does dispersion correlate with these metrics? This strengthens confidence in Phase 1 success criterion.",
  "Cycle 31-32: Run Phase 1 experiments. Conditions 1/2/3, K=10 each (30 total Riemann attempts). Measure dispersion, analyze results. Draft Phase 1 paper: methods (pipeline, config, conditions), results (dispersion measurements, statistical test p-values), implications (H0 supported/not supported). Prepare publication-ready artifact."
]
